slides.title: Connecting the Dots 📏
slides.content:
## What is a Range?

Before we look at loops, we must understand **Ranges**. A Range is simply a sequence of numbers from a starting point to an ending point.

Kotlin makes creating ranges incredibly easy mathematically.

slides.title: The .. Operator ➡️
slides.content:
To define a range, place two dots `..` between the start and end values. 

Note: The `..` operator creates a **closed range**, which means it *includes* both the start and the end number.

quizData.question: Is the ending number (the 10) included when you create a range using `1..10`?

options.text: Maybe, it depends on the compiler.

quizData.explanation: The `..` operator creates a closed-ended range, so both 1 and 10 are inside the sequence.

slides.title: Exclusive Ranges 🚫
slides.content:
If you want to create a range that specifically *excludes* the end number, use the keyword `..<` or `until`.

This is extremely useful later when working with Collections like Arrays.
slides.codeSnippet:
val exclusive = 1 until 10

println(10 in exclusive) // Returns false!

quizData.question: Which keyword creates a range that *does not* include the very last number?
quizData.explanation: The `until` keyword creates half-open ranges, meaning the upper limit is excluded.
